one_line_takeaway: A competent but attack-only empirical demonstration that ~5% label-flipping/image-swap poisoning severely degrades a ResNet50 (accuracy -27.2 pts) and a Random Forest fraud detector (accuracy -22.7 pts, fraud F1 0.83 to 0.42) - but despite its 'Detecting and Preventing' title, it proposes and evaluates no defense.
key_finding: Poisoning a small fraction of training data causes large, statistically significant degradation, and in the fraud-detection case overall accuracy is a deceptive metric that hides collapse of the minority (fraud) class. ResNet50/CIFAR-10 accuracy fell 27.2 percentage points (92.3% to 65.1%, p < 0.001) with a 320% increase in cat/dog misclassifications and per-class F1 drops of 0.33 (cat) and 0.34 (dog). The insurance Random Forest lost 22.7 points of accuracy (97.2% to 74.5%), but the more revealing signal is fraud-class F1 0.83 to 0.42, recall 0.88 to 0.47, precision 0.79 to 0.38, AUC 0.95 to 0.72, and false-positive rate 0.5% to 2.8% - i.e. the model keeps ~74% headline accuracy while its actual fraud-catching ability roughly halves.
core_contribution: An empirical measurement study of how two data-poisoning techniques degrade AI models in two domains: label flipping plus image replacement against a ResNet50 image classifier on CIFAR-10, and label flipping against a Random Forest fraud detector on a tabular insurance-claims dataset. The paper's realized contribution is the controlled clean-vs-poisoned degradation measurement (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC, false-positive rate, misclassification growth, and training dynamics). Note: despite the title 'Detecting and Preventing Data Poisoning Attacks' and abstract claims of 'novel algorithms' and defenses (anomaly detection, adversarial training, ensemble learning) that supposedly restore 15-20% accuracy, no detection or prevention method is specified in the methodology or evaluated in the results. The paper delivers an attack-impact study only; the detection/prevention framing is aspirational rather than demonstrated.
threat_model: Implicit and never formalized by the authors. The adversary is assumed to have write access to the training set and corrupts a small fraction of it before model training: on CIFAR-10, 500 images are swapped between the 'cat' and 'dog' classes and ~5% of labels are flipped; on the insurance dataset, ~5% of fraud/non-fraud labels are flipped. Attacker knowledge, capability bounds, stealth constraints, and goal (targeted vs availability) are not defined; the experiments effectively simulate an availability/label-flipping attacker with direct training-data access. The threat model should be treated as under-specified.
limitations: Major claim-vs-evidence gap: the abstract advertises detection/prevention techniques and 'novel algorithms' with 15-20% accuracy restoration and ensemble-based false-positive reduction, yet the methodology and results contain no defense whatsoever - the paper only measures attack damage. Only two datasets, so findings are dataset-specific (the authors acknowledge this). Poison rate is essentially fixed at ~5% (plus 500 swapped images) with no dose-response sweep and no adaptive/stealthy adversary. The insurance dataset is anonymized and un-sourced, so the fraud experiment is not reproducible. Text has internal inconsistencies and typos (e.g. '27%' vs 27.2, '22%' vs 22.7, 'trunk' for 'truck', 'REALTED WORKS') that undercut rigor. Related-work defense results (e.g. ref [4]'s 85% attack-success reduction) belong to cited papers, not this study.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (60,000 32x32 color images, 10 classes, 50,000 train / 10,000 test)
Insurance Claims dataset (anonymized tabular fraud/non-fraud records; ~1,000,000 records)
quantitative_results: ResNet50 / CIFAR-10 accuracy: 92.3% +/-0.5 (clean) -> 65.1% +/-1.2 (poisoned), a 27.2 percentage-point drop (p < 0.001)
Poisoned CIFAR-10 model overfit earlier (epoch 15 vs 25 baseline); validation loss plateaued at 0.98 vs 0.32 clean
Insurance Random Forest accuracy: 97.2% +/-0.3 (clean) -> 74.5% +/-0.8 (poisoned), a 22.7 percentage-point drop (p < 0.001)
Poisoning injection rates: CIFAR-10 = 500 cat/dog images swapped + 5% labels flipped; insurance = 5% of labels flipped
baselines_compared: Clean-data ResNet50 (image-classification baseline vs its poisoned counterpart)
Clean-data Random Forest classifier (fraud-detection baseline vs its poisoned counterpart)
